title: Lighting sources in the United Kingdom
subtitle: The share of lighting across the United Kingdom, sourced from different energy sources.
caption:
Lighting sources in the United Kingdom
The share of lighting across the United Kingdom, sourced from different energy sources.

This stacked area chart tracks the changing composition of lighting energy sources in the United Kingdom from 1700 to 2025, with the vertical axis measuring the percentage share from 0% to 100%. The legend identifies five distinct categories: Candles, Whale oil, Kerosene, Gas, and Electricity. For the first century of the timeline, Candles occupy the entire vertical space, representing a near-total monopoly on lighting from 1700 through the early 1800s. A transition begins in the 19th century as Whale oil enters the mix, visible as a grey band that expands rapidly between 1800 and 1850, eventually accounting for roughly 40% of the total share before receding. Kerosene appears as a red layer in the mid-19th century, holding a significant portion of the market between 1860 and 1920, fluctuating between 10% and 20% before disappearing entirely by the mid-20th century. Gas lighting emerges as a purple band around 1850, growing to become the dominant source for several decades, peaking in share around 1900 before declining. The most recent structural shift involves Electricity, shown in blue, which enters the chart in the late 19th century and follows a steep upward trajectory. By 1950, Electricity surpasses all other sources combined, and from 1970 onward, the blue area fills the entire chart to the 100% line, indicating the complete displacement of Candles, Whale oil, Kerosene, and Gas. The data shows a sequential replacement of technologies where new sources rise to dominance before being superseded by the next innovation, culminating in the total electrification of lighting.
